Final Step: Narrowing Down To Just Five Companies

In this step, we construct three separate lists of five stocks each, with different sets of goals, dividend income, and risk levels.

The lists are:

1) Relatively Safe (Low-yield) Dividend list,

2) Moderately High Dividend List,

3) Ultra High Dividend List, and

4) A combined list of the above three (duplicates removed).

Out of the top 40, we make our judgment calls to make these three lists, so basically, the selections are based on our research and perceptions. So, while most of the filtering until now was based on automated criteria, the last step is pretty much subjective. We try to make each of the three lists highly diversified among various sectors and industry segments, and try to ensure that the safety of dividends matches the overall risk profile of the group. We certainly encourage readers to do further research on the highlighted names.

Nonetheless, here are our three final lists for this month:

Final A-List (Relatively Safe Income):

Criteria for this group:

  • The average yield for the group is between 2.75% and 4.25%.
  • At least three or four names with an "A" rating (from S&P)
  • Solid track record of dividends and growth.

Average yield: 4.26%

  • (ADP)
  • (UNH)
  • (PG)
  • (BX)
  • (PFE)

Table-1A: A-LIST (Conservative Income)

We think this set of five companies (in the A-List) would form a solid diversified group of dividend companies that would be appealing to income-seeking and conservative investors, including retirees and near-retirees. The average yield is 4.26%, which is almost 4x of what the S&P 500 pays. The average dividend history is 39 years. Also, all five companies have an excellent rating of "A-" or higher. If you must need higher dividends, consider the B-List or the C-List, presented later.

As usual, we will provide a brief synopsis of each of the companies selected here; however, we will not go into too many details, as that will make the article unduly long.

ADP (Automatic Data Processing)

There has been panic selling of software stocks in 2026 due to the perceived fear of AI takeover of many of the enterprise functions. Investors have sold off their software stocks, irrespective of the quality. ADP may be one such company that is oversold due to general sentiment. Also, the recent market downturn has pushed prices even further down. We agree, there is a valid argument that advancements in AI could result in improved capabilities of corporations to move payroll processing and other HR related functions in-house, thereby impacting ADP's business. However, the fear appears to be overdone and ignores the company's own potential to use AI effectively to increase its business and reach. However, there is a certain level of uncertainty that will remain for some time.

ADP's yield has gone up considerably in recent times due to an ongoing price correction. The current dividend yield of 3.33% is very favorable compared to its 5-year average of 2%. It is often considered a favorite of dividend investors, given the company's stability and the reliability of the dividend and above-average dividend growth. The current correction may be offering a unique opportunity.

UNH (UnitedHealth Group):

UnitedHealth is an integrated healthcare company that operates in multiple business segments, such as healthcare insurance, pharmacy benefit management, health provider, and health analytics. Until last year, this approach provided an outstanding record of performance and shareholder returns. However, the company fell from grace with many allegations surrounding its healthcare denial practices and controversies around its Medicare Advantage programs. This has led to an antitrust investigation along with an intense regulatory scrutiny targeted at the company's practices.

Due to the ongoing issues, the market price fell sharply in 2025 by nearly 55%, and has been range-bound since then. Over the last year, Morningstar has adjusted its fair value estimate for the company. Still, the current price sits over 35% below Morningstar’s estimate.

Sure, the company is facing many headwinds, and margins in some of the segments may be permanently lower, but patient investors can still benefit from a possible turnaround down the road. In the meantime, a 3.3% dividend yield can help. So, only patient investors should consider it after due diligence.

PG (Procter & Gamble Co)

Procter & Gamble is a well-known and wide-moat company in the consumer staples segment. Its brands are well known, but the company’s growth has been tepid in recent times. Also, the margins have been under pressure due to inflationary trends since 2021. However, all that said, the company enjoys a wide moat with its vast and well-entrenched brands. The company’s stock price is fairly valued (so, not undervalued), but it provides a decent level of nearly 3% dividend yield that is stable. As a whole, it should be a good, large-cap dividend-paying stock to hold in volatile times.

BX (Blackstone Inc):

We included Blackstone in our A-List last month as well. The company is one of the most respected alternative-asset managers. It has roughly $1.2 trillion in AUM (assets under management), out of which roughly over 72% generate fee-based revenue. Its major business/product lines include private equity, real estate (or real assets), private credit, and other alternatives. Blackstone has specialized in revitalizing property assets or companies by various means. Even though the fundraising by alternative asset managers was challenging during 2022-2025 due to elevated interest rates, Blackstone was able to raise $150 billion or more in each of the three past years.

The company's shares have lost over 25% since the beginning of this year, mostly due to adverse industry-wise cyclical headwinds. Some of the decline is related to the recent overall market decline. That said, the company has faced such issues before, and due to its scale and management excellence, it has come out stronger each time. So, it may be a good time to open a position. In the meantime, the company generates a lot of fee-based revenue and pays over 80% of that in the form of dividends. The current dividend yield is 5.44%, but the dividend amount can vary greatly from year to year. The dividend growth for this company can also be patchy, but it has still managed to grow 13% annualized in the last 5-6 years (since 2020). Morningstar assigns it a wide-moat rating in its industry and assigns a fair value nearly 25% above its current price, even though Morningstar has lowered its fair value estimate.

PFE (Pfizer):

We are including Pfizer in the A-list and B-list this month. The reason is obviously the attractive and tempting yield of 6.25%. We always tend to include one company in the A-list with a slightly higher yield. However, we try to minimize the risks. The stock price of PFE has been so beaten down that the recent market correction (due to the Iran war) has not had much impact on its share price.

The health and pharma sector was highly undervalued in 2024/2025, due to many ongoing uncertainties, but those uncertainties are gradually fading away. This has been reflected in the recovery of the sector in 2026 (prior to the market downturn). This has benefited Pfizer's stock price to some extent, and it has gained significantly and much above the lows of 2025. However, the prices and the yield are still attractive. In fact, the yield is still the highest amongst the major pharma companies, but the dividend growth is tepid. Further, Morningstar assigns it a 4-star rating, and the current price is nearly 15% below its estimate of fair value. The company enjoys an excellent credit rating, even though the acquisition of Seagen in 2023 and Metsera in 2025 added to Pfizer's debt.

Pfizer's pipeline has been improving with several successful recent drug launches and acquisitions. There will be some patent losses between 2026 and 2028, but the major ones will be towards the end of 2028. So, the company has ample time and potential to replace them with the new drugs.

Final B-List (High Yield, Moderately Safe):

Criteria for this group:

  • Yield for the group between 4.75% to 6.75%.
  • At least two names with 'A-' (or above) rated.
  • Above-average track record of dividends and growth.

Average yield: 6.56%

  • (ADP)
  • (UNH)
  • (PFE)
  • (ARCC)
  • (CAG)

Note 1: Very often, we include a few low-risk stocks in the B-list and C-list. Also, oftentimes, a stock can appear in multiple lists. This is done on purpose. We try to make each of our lists fairly diversified among different sectors/industry segments of the economy. We try to include a few of the highly conservative names in the high-yield list to make the overall group much safer.

Table-1B: B-LIST (High Yield)

=====

In the B-List, the overall risk profile of the group becomes slightly elevated compared to the A-List. That said, the group (as a whole) will likely provide safe dividends for many years. This list offers an average yield of 6.6% and an average of 33 years of dividend history and 12 years (average) of consecutive growth.

Some of our high-yield selections often repeat in the B-list and C-list.

ARCC (Ares Capital)

ARCC is one of the largest BDCs (Business Development Companies) in terms of market capitalization and net asset value. The company invests in small and medium-sized companies and has a diversified portfolio of investments in over 500 companies. Besides, ARCC has a proven record of performing reasonably well both in high and low-interest-rate environments. The company offers a stable dividend. The current environment is somewhat challenging to say the least, and that's why the valuation has come down significantly and offers a good opportunity for long-term investors.

It generates a solid amount of NII (net Investment income), which currently exceeds well above the $1.2 billion mark on an annual basis. ARCC currently offers a highly attractive yield of roughly 10.7%. The most recent NII for the last quarter was $0.52 a share, which was still above the quarterly dividend of $0.48 a share, though the margin of coverage is much lower.

CAG (Conagra Brands)

Conagra offers a very attractive dividend yield of 9.2%. At first, we should be alarmed by that level of dividend yield from a consumer staples company. So, apparently, there are risks that investors should be aware of before they make investments in this company. The company's stock price has been in a constant downtrend all of this year and has lost more than 50% since 2023. The stock has lost over 33% in 2025 alone while the S&P500 gained 19% in the same time period. However, most of the downside appears to be priced in, and further downside may be limited, if any, unless the broader market keeps declining due to the geopolitical situation.

Now, some reality with negatives and risks. The company does not enjoy any moat and is facing stiff competition and pricing pressures from private labels. Moreover, its balance sheet is not in a very strong situation with net debt/adjusted-EBITDA of nearly 3.8x. Also, the dividend payout is somewhat elevated at 73%; however, the company is at least targeting to bring it down to 55% in the next few years. With that outlook, there is high income with potentially elevated risks. Only income investors with high risk tolerance should consider this stock.

Final C-LIST (Yield-Hungry, Less Safe):

Criteria for this group:

  • The average yield for the group is between 7.0% to 8.0%
  • All names with investment-grade ratings.
  • Some potential for growth.

Average yield: 7.92%

  • (BX)
  • (MPLX)
  • (GLPI)
  • (ARCC)
  • (CAG)

Notes:

Note 1: Oftentimes, a stock can appear in multiple lists. We try to include one or two conservative names in the high-yield list to make the overall group much safer.

Table-1C: C-LIST (Yield-Hungry, Elevated Risk)

NOTE: MPLX is structured as a partnership (not a corporation) and issues a K-1 tax form instead of the usual 1099-Div. Please use due diligence.

=====

It is apparent from the selections that this list (C-List) is for yield-hungry DGI investors. The yield goes up to 7.9%. However, please note that this list is not for conservative investors. As you can see, the average credit rating of this set of companies is much lower than the A-List or even the B-List. Dividends are moderately safe in this list, but not nearly as safe as in the A-List or even the B-list. At least a couple of companies in this list may have an elevated level of risk, as they are sometimes turnaround stories. We urge due diligence to determine if it would suit your personal situation. Nothing comes for free, so there will be more risk involved with this group. Even then, it's a diversified group spread among five different industry segments.

MPLX (MPLX LP)

MPLX LP makes it to our B-list and C-list on a regular basis. It is a highly attractive name from the midstream energy industry. Please note that the company has a Partnership structure and issues a K-1 tax form (partnership income) instead of the usual 1099 in case of regular dividends.

We have covered MPLX multiple times in our previous articles in this monthly series. Hence, we will skip the details here. MPLX regularly features on our C-list (and sometimes on the B-list) due to its high income, stable business, and decent dividend growth. Also, there are other good alternatives, such as Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) or Energy Transfer (ET).

GLPI (Gaming and Leisure Properties):

GLPI is a real estate investment property trust, and it specializes in acquiring, financing, owning, and leasing real estate properties related to the gaming and leisure industry. GLPI leases its properties to gaming operators in triple-net lease arrangements.

GLPI came into existence as a REIT in 2013 due to the spinoff from Penn National Gaming. Since then, it has continually expanded its operations at a steady pace and now owns a portfolio of 69 gaming properties with over 15,000 hotel rooms spanning across 20 states. Most GLPI properties are in the northeastern and south-central parts of the United States. Its portfolio is considered high quality, with several tenants ranked number one in their respective markets. The only other publicly traded REIT in the gaming industry is VICI Properties (VICI), which is much larger than GLPI in terms of its assets and market cap.

GLPI's occupancy rates have been extremely good at 100% for most of its existence. It has a total of eight unique tenants; however, over 87% of the rents are received from just four tenants/operators. So, that may look like a huge negative, but it has had zero rent defaults so far. In addition, this is just the nature of the gaming industry, as there are only a limited number of operators.

The company is currently paying a quarterly dividend of $0.78 per share, which increased from $0.76 per share in the year-ago period (and $0.73 two years ago). So, its dividend is $3.12 a year, and the AFFO in 2025 was $3.88, covering the dividend at 1.24x. The AFFO guidance for 2026 is between $4.06 to $4.11.

GLPI is a very stable cash-flow generator and a reliable dividend payer. With the exception of 2020, when it cut the dividends by about 10%, it has raised the dividends almost every year. It also pays a small amount in special dividends sometimes. The average dividend growth has been in the low single digits over the last 5 years and 10 years. The current yield is very attractive at 6.95% and much better than its five-year average of roughly 6%.

Note: We would like to caution that each company comes with certain risks and concerns. Sometimes, these risks are real, but other times, they may be a bit overblown and temporary. So, it's always recommended to do further research and due diligence.

What If We Were to Combine the Three Lists?

If we combine the three lists and remove the duplicates (because of combining), we would be left with nine names.

Two-time duplicates: ADP, ARCC, BX, CAG, PFE, and UNH (6 duplicates).

After removing these SIX duplicates, we are left with nine names.

The combined list is highly diversified in as many as nine different industry segments. However, there may be multiple names for some of the sectors. The stats for the group of 9 are as follows:

Average yield: 6.2%

Average discount (from 52WK High): -26.7%

Average 5-yr dividend growth: 7.9%.

Average Payout Ratio: 72%.

Average Total Quality Score: 65.4.

Table 3:

Conclusion:

In this article, we have presented three groups of dividend-paying stocks (five each) with different goals in mind to suit the varying needs of a wider audience. Even though the risk profile of each group is different, each group in itself is fairly balanced and diversified.

This month, the first group yields 4.26%, while the second group elevates the yield to 6.56%. We also presented a C-list for yield-hungry investors with a 7.92% yield. The combined group (all three lists combined with duplication removed) offers an even more diversified group with nine positions and a 6.16% yield.

data.title: Zoetis: Deep Value Persists
data.description:
Co-authored by Kody's Dividends

Are you more of a dog or a cat person?

It's not an uncommon question within the United States, and most people have an immediate answer. According to the American Veterinary Medical Association, Americans favor dogs over cats. The same source reports that over 87 million dogs and 76 million cats are owned by Americans. This equates to 42.6% of households owning a dog and 32.6% owning a cat. Ironically, cat owners usually own more cats per home than dog owners do.

With well over 150 million cats and dogs owned within the United States, demand for their food, toys, and medical care remains a consistent market for companies to address effectively.

As a dividend investor focused on maximizing total returns and buying growing dividends, I am always on the lookout for attractively priced companies in the market that meet every type of persistent consumer need.

Today, I want to look at a company meeting pet owners where they have a consistent need: the medical needs of their pets.

Let's dive in!

Potential Blockbuster Candidates Can Drive Continued Growth

Our investment thesis in Zoetis (ZTS) remains similar to when we last highlighted it in January: the company has hundreds of product lines in seven major product categories, which provides it with broad diversification.

Unsurprisingly, the company is diversified in more than just its product lines: geographically, almost 55% of its $9.47 billion in 2025 revenue was derived in the U.S., with the remaining 45% being generated in international markets (page 3 of 110 of ZTS's 10-K Filing).

In Q4 2025, the company proved its resilience. ZTS's revenue edged 3% higher year-over-year to $2.39 billion in the quarter. This came in about $25 million ahead of Seeking Alpha's analyst consensus during the quarter. While the U.S. market experienced softness (organic revenue was flat), international markets thrived, posting 7% organic growth for the quarter.

ZTS's key growth driver was again the powerhouse parasiticide franchise, Simparica, with the Trio product topping $1 billion in annual U.S. sales alone (3% operational growth in Q4 2025). Dermatology franchises Apoquel and Cytopoint continued to be major contributors, but growth slowed to just 1% in Q4 2025 ($426 million in Q4 2025 revenue).

ZTS's adjusted diluted EPS rose by 5.7% over the year-ago period to $1.48 in the fourth quarter. This surpassed the analyst consensus by $0.08. The company's non-GAAP net profit margin held above 27% during the quarter. Combined with a 2.8% reduction in the share count in Q4 2025, that's what helped adjusted diluted EPS growth outpace revenue growth for the quarter.

Looking ahead, two commercial launches are the most notable catalysts in 2026. Last December, the next-generation osteoarthritis medicine for cats called Portela was approved in Canada (following approval in the European Union). Unlike Solensia, which required monthly injections, this is designed to provide three months of relief with a single shot. The longer-acting profile is a big compliance win because many cat owners find frequent vet visits stressful.

Last November, the next-generation osteoarthritis medicine for dogs known as Lenivia was approved by the European Commission. This should also yield improvements in compliance because only one injection is required every three months versus Librela, which is a monthly injection.

ZTS's BBB+ S&P credit rating with a stable outlook also provides it with a low cost of capital to seek out bolt-on acquisitions to further complement the business. The latest example was the $160 million acquisition of Neogen's animal genomics business announced earlier this month to enhance its capabilities to deliver predictive insights, individualized care, and more value to customers.

That's why the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is for adjusted diluted EPS to grow by 8.4% annually through 2028, off a 2025 base of $6.41.

ZTS's 1.8% dividend yield is reasonably attractive. This is especially the case when considering that its adjusted diluted EPS payout ratio is likely to be in the low-30% range for 2026. That should set ZTS up to lengthen its 12-year dividend growth streak with at least 8% or 9% annual dividend growth (for a Chowder number of at least 10 or 11).

ZTS is also a clear value. From the current $117 share price (April 2nd, 2026), the stock is trading at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 16.5. For more color, this is about half of the FAST Graphs' 10-year average P/E ratio of 31.8.

Of course, we don't believe that the fair value multiple in the years ahead will be even close to 30. In our view, a more attainable fair value P/E ratio is around 23.

This factors in the fact that ZTS's 8.4% forward annual adjusted EPS growth consensus is meaningfully lower than the 10-year CAGR of 13.7%. Even so, the forward 12-month multiple is so low that the stock is priced at over a 25% discount to our fair value per share estimate of $164.

If ZTS returns to fair value and lives up to growth projections, it could generate a 42% total return by the end of March 2027. Through 2031, ZTS could log 14.86% annual total returns.

Key risks to the company center on its Librela and Solensia osteoarthritis pain franchises. As we discussed in January, there were more than 12,000 adverse drug events reported in dogs for Librela and another 6,000-plus incidents reported for cats on Solensia between May 2023 and March 2025.

Now, reported incidents and causal links are two entirely different things. Still, if vets become skeptical about prescribing the medicines or pet owners refuse the prescription, that could weigh on the franchise's sales. Fortunately, ZTS does have a diversified product portfolio to at least partially counter this risk.

Another risk to the company is a cooling market. A whitepaper from Vetsource, analyzing transaction data from thousands of veterinary practices, found that year-over-year visits were down 3.1% in 2025.

This demonstrates that the post-pandemic boom in pet ownership has cooled off, with many pet owners showing higher price sensitivity for routine care due to tighter household budgets. That caps the company's ability to drive growth through price increases.

Conclusion

ZTS is an undervalued and attractive opportunity to invest in the medical care of animals all around the world. There is a persistent and consistent need for treatments to avoid ticks, fleas, heartworms, and other pests that are attracted to domesticated animals. Furthermore, ZTS has a portfolio of other medical offerings that it's able to use to try to aid in the betterment of the lives of pets. Make no mistake, ZTS is not a charity that is seeking to simply help every animal in the world. It's a business driving to make profits, use those profits to reward their shareholders, and conduct further research to find additional profitable avenues of medical care for animals.

ZTS also owns a wide database of animal DNA through its BasePaws DNA tests, enabling it to see where there may be persistent issues in house pets all around the world. This DNA database is of extreme value and enables ZTS to spot trends or patterns in the DNA that is sent to them from paying customers.

At the end of the day, finding dividends and total returns from companies that meet consumers where there is a persistent need and meet those consumers effectively is a massive benefit to your portfolio. Their Simparica Trio product line is extremely popular among pet owners and is a massive profit driver for ZTS. Likewise, it can be a massive reward in your own personal portfolio as well.

data.title: Delta Air Lines Will Potentially Lead In This Year Of Chaos
data.description:
Delta Air Lines (DAL) is scheduled to kick off the U.S. airline earnings season on Wednesday April 8. The stock market and particularly the airline industry has been whipsawed by every news item that has come out about the war between the U.S. and Israel against Iran. Regardless of what happens in the near term with the war, there have already been significant impacts to the availability and cost of petroleum including refinery products, with diesel and jet fuel being particularly hard hit. Against that backdrop, DAL will, once again, lead the industry earnings reporting cycle and provide insight into not just the first quarter of 2026 – esp. since the war started with two-thirds of that quarter completed – but also provide guidance as to how they expect the second quarter and the remainder of the year to play out.

First, let’s review DAL’s performance for the fourth quarter and full year 2025. For the latest quarter, Delta beat on both GAAP earnings and revenue. For 2025, DAL reported a 9.8% pre-tax margin, or 33% more pre-tax income, on $63.4 billion of revenue, the highest for any airline in the world. Operating cash flow was $8.3 billion with $14.1 billion in total debt and financial lease obligations. 2025 was a good year for Delta and its financial position to the rest of the U.S. airline industry improved; Delta execs note that they account for 55% of the profits of the U.S. airline industry.

A strong demand environment

Delta provided strong guidance for the first quarter when it reported its 4th quarter and full year 2025 earnings. At that time, its guidance for 2026 was earnings per share growth of 20% at the midpoint over 2025 levels. Delta as well as a number of other airlines presented at the JP Morgan Industrials conference on March 17 which provided interesting insights given that the Iran war had been in effect for nearly three weeks. Even though each of the big 3 U.S. global carriers, also including American (AAL) and United (UAL), stated that they expect fuel costs to reduce earnings by $400 million in the first quarter, the biggest news was that demand was very strong, sentiment heard not just from the global carriers but across the entire U.S. industry. Several factors might explain the increased revenue growth this year during the first quarter. Easter is two weeks earlier than it was in 2025 which has undoubtedly pushed some demand into the first quarter. In addition, there are strong indications that some passengers accelerated their travel bookings when the war broke out and fuel prices began to increase, certain that fare increases would follow, which has been the case. Delta as well as other airlines noted that fare increases have happened far faster after the latest war began than in most other events that have necessitated fare increases. There is a general optimism that fare increases can be passed along – at least in the near to medium term.

In addition, capacity has begun to come out of the U.S. airline system, esp. in domestic markets. The biggest decrease in capacity has come from Spirit Airlines which is operating under chapter 11 bankruptcy protection and recently rejected leases on a number of aircraft and announced plans to operate an airline less than half the size of its previous size. Several smaller airlines join Spirit in capacity reductions while the largest carriers except for Southwest are growing faster than industry average with American adding the most capacity.

The timing of the Middle East conflict meant that most spring break related traffic was already booked – so the challenge was that higher cost fuel would be consumed for tickets that had largely been purchased well before the conflict began to push up fuel prices. A good portion of summer international travel has also been booked or is in the booking process right now so airlines have not removed a great deal of international capacity. U.S. airlines have not reinstated flights to Israel – which Delta and United served – or to other points in the Middle East.

U.S. airlines have increasingly seen a shift to the second quarter as their best quarter, weakening the third quarter, in part because many schools are back in session by early August. April and May are now part of the peak international travel season esp. for older travelers who do not plan their travel around children. There is a decent chance that further capacity cuts will take place for the third quarter and beyond if fuel prices remain high; not only will demand decrease at higher fare levels, but a significant amount of airline capacity will not be profitable at elevated fuel costs. There are indications that airlines are slowing their hiring for the second half of the year and preparing for the possibility of removing older aircraft for service even as newer and more fuel- efficient aircraft are due to be delivered. Against a strong demand environment, Delta and the industry is generally optimistic through the first half of the year with the outlook for the second half of the year to be determined.

Fuel cost management matters for 2026

The key issue that will determine the course of the U.S. airline industry in 2026 will be the cost of jet fuel – and Delta holds a distinct advantage in managing fuel costs because of its ownership of the Trainer refinery outside Philadelphia in a unique move for the industry. Although many airlines have hedged fuel costs, none have been successful and Southwest (LUV) unwound its fuel hedges last year as part of its financial restructuring. Against a backdrop of unsuccessful hedges, Delta acquired a 185,000 bbl/day refinery which it tuned to maximize jet fuel production; it gained access to pipelines to its airports in New York City and other airports in the Northeast where the jet fuel from the refinery is used while it trades the remaining refined products including gasoline to other oil companies for jet fuel which is used elsewhere on Delta’s network. The refinery directly and indirectly provides approximately 75% of Delta’s fuel needs.

The refinery’s primary intent is to offset fuel costs for the airline and DAL has been successful in doing that over the 14 years that they have operated the refinery. The refinery’s primary mechanism for lowering Delta’s fuel costs is by reducing the crack spread for jet fuel and diesel; the crack spread is the cost to refine crude oil into specific products. Kerosene, diesel and jet fuel are all considered middle distillates and the crack spread or refiner’s margin for all of those products has soared since the war began. Although most light sweet crude, the kind that the U.S. predominantly produces, can produce about 3 to 4 gallons of jet fuel per barrel of crude, an even larger part of the barrel can be refined into diesel. Delta is likely benefitting now financially more because of the elevated cost of diesel than from its savings on jet fuel costs.

The refinery has saved Delta several billion dollars in fuel costs over its 14 years; the most recent period of significant savings came in 2022 after Russia invaded Ukraine and global fuel supplies became unbalanced because of sanctions that were put on Russian oil In 2022, the refinery saved DAL $777 million or 23 cents/gallon with half of that amount in 2023 even though the refinery was offline for a turnaround which took place in the latter half of 2023. Even if the current war ends tomorrow and the Strait of Hormuz is reopened, crude oil supplies have been significantly reduced around the world and there are significant shortages of refined products including jet fuel in many parts of the world that will not likely be corrected in 2026 and perhaps not even in 2027. It is very possible that this war could reduce DAL’s fuel costs by another billion dollars relative to competitors, providing a significant competitive advantage.

Premium revenue continues to deliver

Much of the airline industry is still working through the post-covid environment that has been marked by a separation of the industry into winners and those that struggle based on their success at gaining high value passengers. Delta’s financial ascent in the decade of the 2010s was marked by its success in winning over high value travelers; Delta carries more corporate travel revenue than any other U.S. airline. Delta spent the last decade building out its network in major cities on the east and west coasts of the U.S.; over the past decade, Delta has become the largest airline at Boston, is the largest domestic airline from New York City, and is the largest airline at Los Angeles and second largest at Seattle. These large coastal cities attract outsized credit card spending which has fueled Delta’s partnership with American Express (AXP), adding $8.3 billion of credit card and loyalty program revenue to DAL in 2025 and giving Delta the most valuable airline loyalty program in the world. Delta’s pivot to premium passengers and high margin non-transportation revenue will likely insulate the company from downturns that could possibly come if the economy weakens as a result of higher fuel prices that are passed throughout the economy as well as reduced stock market valuations that limit spending by wealthier Americans.

Macroeconomic weakness is likely

Recent data indicates that economic uncertainty is growing with lower consumer confidence. Even before the war, there was growing dissatisfaction not just with the rate of inflation – which has been reduced from peaks during covid– but the fact that many prices including for food, housing and energy are still high. Airlines that catered to discount-oriented passengers have suffered the most in the post-covid era and most U.S. airlines have shifted to more premium strategies to capture higher value passengers. Southwest (LUV) is engaged in one of the most significant restructurings of its business plan, notable because of the sheer size of its network which is almost entirely domestic. LUV has reworked its route network including reducing its presence in legacy carrier hubs such as Atlanta where Delta operates the world’s largest airline hub. JetBlue (JBLU) remains financially fragile after years of losses; it too is trying to rework its strategies but its trials have allowed Delta to grow where the two overlap including in New York City and Boston. Spirit (SAVEQ) has cut nearly half of its capacity; it overlaps most with Delta in Detroit where DAL is the largest carrier. American has generated some of the industry’s lowest margins over the past decade so has much less ability to withstand cost increases; American and Delta compete for passengers esp. in the eastern U.S. and to/from Florida. High fuel costs and a potential weakening of the economy are most likely to negatively impact carriers that have disproportionately larger operations in Delta strength markets. Nonetheless, Delta is not immune to macroeconomic impacts.

In addition to macroeconomic effects, government handling of aviation has been chaotic esp. involving TSA security checkpoint operations, a result of repeated government shutdowns impacting the Dept. of Homeland Security. The FAA has also taken a more aggressive approach to addressing safety concerns in the aftermath of the crash of an Air Canada regional jet with a firetruck at New York's LaGuardia airport where DAL has a major hub operation.

Delta is viewed favorably by industry analysts

The recent movements in airline stocks highlights market understanding that DAL is better positioned to ride out this crisis than other airlines. They, along with other airlines, might be forced to reduce cleaning up their balance sheets but Delta is very likely going to be a position to make competitive gains while reporting profits that will grow as a percentage of the U.S. and global industry. Wall Street analysts are particularly bullish about Delta with the highest percentage of BUY or STRONG BUY in the industry.

Delta and Southwest are the only two U.S. airlines with investment grade balance sheets and both also pay dividends.

The guidance for the remainder of 2026, if DAL provides it, as well as their guidance for the second quarter and their commentary about the demand environment and DAL’s ability to manage costs, esp. how much the refinery is expected to contribute, will be far more important than the first quarter financials that the company provides. Airlines as a group as well as other transportation companies will likely underperform broader indices in 2026 although general market sentiment continues to rapidly rise and fall based on developments in the Iran war.

Delta is likely to be one of the better performing transportation companies perhaps even on a global basis. DAL stock remains a BUY.

data.title: Essential Utilities: Not Too Late To Buy Now
data.description:
As a quality-oriented investor, I’m a fan of companies with consistent track records of dividend growth. In an era of rapid market shifts and high-interest rates, the Dividend Champions offer some intriguing options, in my opinion. These are publicly traded companies with at least 25 years of payout raises to their credit.

Within this elite group of dividend payers, regulated water utilities have carved out a unique niche. They tend to offer a mix of high barriers to entry (it’s very expensive to build out an extensive infrastructure network), reliable demand for utility services, and predictable regulatory frameworks.

That leads me into my focus for today, which is Essential Utilities (WTRG). When I last covered WTRG with a Buy rating in January, I covered it from the angle of what it would look like if its pending merger with American Water Works (AWK) closed. I was encouraged by an improvement in the EPS growth profile from 5% to 7% independently to 7% to 9% in such a case. A one-notch improvement in the S&P credit rating to A was another positive. In closing, both regulated water utilities were undervalued.

A couple of months later, I’m going to lean more toward the deal not being completed; the odds are probably two or three to one that it will be cleared. One big step in this direction was that in February, 99% of AWK’s shares voted to approve the deal, and 95% of WTRG’s voted in support of it. It still must win over state regulators, so it’s not a done deal yet.

On its own, WTRG is poised to keep generating 5% to 7% annual adjusted EPS growth. The company’s A- S&P credit rating keeps its cost of capital low. That allows it to bridge the capex that’s not covered by operating cash flow and gives it the capital needed for accretive bolt-on acquisitions. Clinching my continued Buy rating, shares are still priced at a double-digit percentage discount to my fair value estimate.

Steady Business Growth Ahead

On February 25th, WTRG released its earnings report for the fourth quarter ended December 31st, 2025. The company’s total operating revenue rose by 15.7% year-over-year to $699.1 million during the quarter.

What contributed to WTRG’s double-digit percentage topline growth rate in the fourth quarter? The biggest driver for the utility for the quarter was the successful execution of its regulatory strategy. Throughout 2025, WTRG secured rate awards and surcharges across both the Water and Natural Gas segments.

The former segment recorded $329.4 million in operating revenue during the fourth quarter, which was up 8.0% over the year-ago period. Increases in water and wastewater rates were the primary contributing factor to these results.

The latter segment reported $361.3 million in operating revenue in the fourth quarter, which was a 23% year-over-year growth rate. Rate case successes here and specialized surcharges like the Infrastructure Improvement Charge were instrumental to these results. Favorable weather also benefited the Natural Gas segment. The final component of the segment’s outsized topline growth was higher purchased gas costs. As a regulated utility, WTRG passes these costs onto customers without a markup. While this is neutral to net income, it artificially boosts the topline in times of higher commodity prices.

Shifting to the bottom line, WTRG recorded $0.47 in diluted EPS for the fourth quarter. That was down 29.9% over the year-ago period. This beat Seeking Alpha’s analyst consensus during the quarter by $0.07. Rather than anything concerning, the reason for the drop in net income had to do with the absence of a one-time gain from asset sales that occurred in Q4 2024. Higher operations and maintenance expenses from a larger rate base played a role as well.

Moving forward, WTRG has plenty of growth tailwinds.

For one, there’s the fact that the regulated utility is planning to invest $1.7 billion in 2026. That would be an acceleration from the record $1.4 billion allocated in 2025. This will be used to replace aging water mains and gas pipelines, PFAS remediation, and accelerate the rollout of Advanced Metering Infrastructure and AI-driven predictive maintenance to enhance billing accuracy and operational efficiency. This will drive further rate base growth for WTRG, which will propel operating revenue higher over time.

Additionally, the company has $101.9 million of requested annualized revenue increases pending via rate cases in North Carolina, Texas, Ohio, Virginia, and New Jersey. The company has already had $12.4 million of annualized revenue increases from other rate cases that went into effect in January.

One final tailwind for WTRG is its A- S&P credit rating with a stable outlook. This low cost of capital allowed it to issue $500 million of 10-year senior notes at 5.125% in March. In this rate environment, those are attractive terms. This is important because it helps the company to execute bolt-on acquisitions of water and wastewater systems in its service territories (acquisitions amounting to $300 million and over 200,000 customers are currently pending in various transactions for WTRG).

This is why the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is for adjusted diluted EPS to grow by 1.8% in 2026 to $2.24. For 2027, another 7.1% jump in adjusted diluted EPS to $2.40 is anticipated. In 2028, an additional 5% increase in adjusted diluted EPS is being projected (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all info in this subhead was according to WTRG’s Q4 2025 Earnings Press Release and WTRG’s Q4 2025 Earnings Presentation).

Solid Upside To Fair Value

Since January, WTRG’s shares have logged a 5% total return. That convincingly beat the S&P 500 index’s (SP500) -6% total return over the period. Even so, there’s still value to be had with WTRG right now.

The stock is trading at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 17.9. That’s up only slightly from the prior value of 17.7. For more perspective, this is around three standard deviations less than the 10-year average P/E ratio of 25.2 (down slightly from 25.6 as the higher valuation reference year of 2015 was replaced with the lower valuation reference year of 2025), according to FAST Graphs.

Moving forward, I would argue that a fair value P/E ratio for WTRG remains right around two standard deviations below the 10-year average multiple. Independently, the company’s 4.9% annual forward adjusted diluted EPS growth consensus is about the same as its 10-year CAGR of 5.7%. That supports my argument that WTRG’s growth prospects are intact.

Just like in January, though, the current interest rate environment is positioned to remain a major drag on the water utility’s fair value P/E ratio. The current 4.3% 10-year U.S. Treasury yield (US10Y) is much higher than the 10-year average yield of roughly 2.7%, per Macrotrends.

I continue to think that the 10-year yield will consistently be in the low-4% range in the coming years. Applying a roughly 100 basis point spread to WTRG’s earnings yield relative to the 10-year yield (similar to the 10-year average spread of 100 to 125 basis points), this is how I arrive at a fair value multiple of 20.

As things stand, the calendar year 2026 is 25% complete. That suggests there’s another 75% of 2026 and 25% of 2027 yet to come over the next 12 months. This yields a forward 12-month adjusted diluted EPS input of $2.28.

Plugging that into my fair value P/E ratio of 20, I compute a fair value of $46 a share. Compared to the current $41 share price (as of April 2nd, 2026), that equates to a 10% discount to fair value.

If WTRG meets growth expectations and reverts to fair value, it could generate a 17% total return by the end of March 2027. Through 2031, the water utility could put up 10% annual total returns. If its deal with AWK is ultimately completed, the return potential would be about the same (higher growth prospects would compensate for the lower yield).

The Payout Can Keep Rising

WTRG’s 3.4% forward dividend yield comes in about 20 basis points above the 3.2% median forward yield of the utilities sector. That represents a shift from January, in which the two were at parity with each other. This explains how WTRG earns B- and C+ grades from Seeking Alpha’s Quant System for forward dividend yield and overall dividend yield (both of which are the same as they were in January).

The company’s dividend is also reasonably sustainable. This is evidenced by a payout ratio that’s likely to be in the low-60% range in 2026. According to the Zen Research Terminal, that would be comfortably less than the 75% payout ratio set forth by rating agencies as the upper-limit for the industry. This would also be perfectly within its targeted payout ratio range of between 60% and 65%.

As a result, the Zen Research Terminal pegs the risk of a dividend cut as an independent company at only 0.7% within the next 12 months. In the next average recession, that risk rises to 2%. It’s again worth noting that in the likely event that WTRG’s merger with AWK is approved, the dividend will be cut by about 26%, according to the math from fellow SA analyst Ian Bezek.

Should the deal fall through, that sets up WTRG to build on its 34-year dividend growth streak. This is already almost quadruple the sector median of 8.9 years, which is enough to garner an A grade from the Quant System for the consecutive years of dividend growth metric.

The Quant System is projecting 5.7% annual forward dividend per share growth for WTRG. This would be above the sector median of 5.4%. In my view, that’s a realistic expectation for dividend growth since the payout ratio can allow WTRG to grow the dividend in line with adjusted diluted EPS for the foreseeable future. This accounts for the Quant System’s B grade for the metric.

Risks To Consider

In either form, WTRG is a regulated utility with a clear path to growth in the years ahead. Still, it faces risks that should be watched in the future.

WTRG and AWK decisively approved the merger in February. However, the deal isn’t done yet. The next regulatory hurdles for the pending deal to clear are approvals from state public utility commissions, including Pennsylvania and Illinois.

As I noted in January, the tie-up of the nation’s two biggest utilities won’t come without its share of controversy. In many jurisdictions, WTRG and AWK often compete against each other in buyouts of local assets, such as in Chester, Pennsylvania, with the city’s local sewage facility up for sale. Combining these two will reduce competition for government-owned utility buyouts, which could result in better deals for a combined entity.

That’s precisely the risk that could hold this deal back, though. In exchange for giving the deal the green light, state regulators will likely require concessions such as potential rate freezes or mandating a portion of merger savings be passed directly to customers.

If the merger is complete, the combined company would still have to navigate the integration phase. This carries the risk of customer service disruptions and important personnel leaving the company.

Another risk is the refinancing of older, cheaper debt with more expensive debt in this higher-rate environment. In an environment where more Americans are concerned about higher utility bills, it could be a delicate needle for WTRG to thread to recover these higher financing costs in future rate cases.

Summary: A Quality Utility With Decent Return Potential

WTRG is positioned to keep growing at a respectable rate regardless of whether it becomes part of AWK. The water utility’s continued infrastructure investments and room for more bolt-on acquisitions bode well for the future. As does its A- S&P credit rating with a stable outlook. The payout has room to keep growing in line with earnings growth. Lastly, shares remain moderately undervalued. That’s why I’m standing by my Buy rating.

data.title: NiSource: Positioned For Further Data Center-Powered Growth
data.description:
Shares of NiSource (NI) have been a solid performer over the past year, gaining about 17%. In my view, this utility is one of the best positioned to benefit from growing data center electricity usage. While there has been political pushback against data centers over fears of rising electricity costs, NiSource has created structures to isolate these costs, leaving it well positioned to weather any political challenge. Investors appear to be recognizing these strengths, with shares up 13% since I rated them a “buy” in January, even as the broader market has declined. With shares having passed my $45 price target and with updated financials, now is a good time to revisit NI to determine if shares remain a buy or if investors should take profits.

Importantly, in its March investor update, NI reiterated its 8-9% EPS growth target through 2033, though growth will be a bit faster after 2030 as it enjoys the full benefits of its AMZN project. This assumes a 9-11% rate base growth, and there will be a 1-3% headwind from financing costs as NI will be an ongoing issuer of debt and equity to fund a portion of its capital program.

While past success does not guarantee future results, it is worth noting that NI has consistently met or exceeded previous years’ outlooks. This has come without even realizing the benefits of data center demand, which is largely a 2026-2035 phenomenon as these massive projects are built out. NiSource’s ability to deliver and exceed guidance is driven, in my view, by the company’s focus on cap-ex that is recovered within 12 months, providing a clearer line of sight to revenue and less regulatory risk. Because its cap-ex spending is still largely centered around such efforts, I believe the company is positioned to deliver on its targets.

This solid performance continued in 2025; NiSource earned $1.90 in adjusted EPS with Q4 coming in at $0.51 thanks to benefits of regulatory rate capture. I would also note that the company benefited from a cold start to the winter, boosting operating income by $12 million in the quarter and $33 million in the calendar year. This was a nice improvement from 2024 when weather was a $60 million headwind. Weather can be volatile year-to-year but should average out over time.

Looking forward, over the next five years, it will be investing $28 billion to improve and modernize its infrastructure. There is $21 billion of baseline spending, with $6-7 billion for the Amazon project. Under its agreement with Amazon (AMZN), which isolates out the cost of generating electricity for its data center, customers should receive about $1 billion in cost savings or a peak of $7-9 per month off their bill. I expect the Amazon contract to receive regulatory approval by June, with the proceeding progressing as hoped, including a settlement in principle that is consistent with the original arrangement.

As you can see below, the majority of its spending is tied to projects where it gets recovery within 6 months, and 80-90% comes from projects where it recovers the cost within a year. Potential data center deals create long-term upside to cap-ex and rate base growth. However, given the timing of such projects, they are likely to be several years away. More data centers will keep growth higher in the 2030s but do little to shift the next 2-3 years. Increasingly, I am focused on Ohio as a state with meaningful opportunity.

Ohio has taken legislative action to spur development of data centers in the state. Under the new law, large customers like data center can sign bespoke agreements with utility companies for their power, essentially isolating their cost from the rest of the grid. This will allow NiSource’s Ohio utility to engage in the same type of contract its Indiana subsidiary did with Amazon. From a political perspective, this is a very attractive option for state leaders. Given the political toxicity of inflation, with electricity bills already having surged (see below), there is fear of approving data centers and having higher customer bills. That said, states want the construction jobs and investment benefits of these large projects.

These deals give them the best of both worlds with investment in their state plus confidence that customer bills will not be impacted, muting the political risk of inflation. This also has become an acceptable solution for big tech companies. Given the intensity of the race in AI, getting quicker approval is essential, which means solving potential political objections over cost. Plus, so long as they actually use the electricity they anticipate, they ultimately will not bear financial risk. With companies like Amazon and Meta (META) striking such deals across different states, I expect this effort to “isolate” data center costs to be the go-forward norm. This helps de-risk the utilities’ cap-ex plans and is a positive for companies like NiSource.

Critically, NiSource maintains a sound balance sheet, which enables it to undertake this massive capital program and insulates it better from a period of higher-for-longer interest rates. FFO to debt is 16.1%, at the top end of its 14-16% target. As you can see below, the company will be consistently issuing equity to support its cap-ex spending and maintain solid balance sheet metrics. I expect ~$400 million of annual equity issuance under its baseline plan.

This points to about ~1.75% annual dilution. The good news is that the higher NI goes, the fewer shares it needs to issue in order to raise the same amount of capital. As such, the strength of its stock has helped to essentially reduce the dilution cost of its cap-ex program, boosting long-term earnings growth vs. if shares were still below $40. Now, if more data center deals are signed, I expect its equity issuance needs to rise. As a general rule of thumb, each $1 billion of incremental cap-ex is likely to require another ~$200 million of equity issuance.

Shares offer a 2.5% dividend yield, and NiSource has a strong track record as a dividend payer with 14 straight years of growth. Over the past five years, its dividend has grown at a 6% pace. With a solid balance sheet and credible, significant cap-ex program, I view NI as likely to continue growing its dividend, likely by 6-8% over the next 5 years. The company is targeting $2.02-$2.07 in EPS this year. Given its highly regulated nature, I view guidance as highly credible. This winter was abnormally cold in the eastern half of the United States, and that should support higher residential demand in Q1. As such, I am targeting $2.05-$2.10 of EPS this year.

Back in January, I saw the company growing earnings 8-8.5% over the next five years with another data center deal necessary to push growth past 9%. I still expect 8+% growth, and I am cautiously optimistic about future deals with data centers given efforts in Ohio and Pennsylvania to compete for these projects. With ~6-8% dividend growth and a 2.5% starting yield. I see shares likely to generate a long-term return of 8.5-10.5%.

This puts shares right around my ~10% buy threshold, leaving it between a “buy” and a “hold.” While not as compelling as three months ago, I am maintaining NI at a “buy” as I view it as having one of the most favorable regulatory environments and a clear structure to enable data center investment. Especially in this uncertain macro environment, NI’s regulated cash flows are attractive, and I see shares returning about 10% over the next year.

data.title: Semiconductors Winners And Losers At The Start Of Q2 2026
data.description:
The semiconductor sector got off to a great start, but it lost most of the gains by the end of Q1 2026 as the sector stumbled towards the latter part of the quarter. Semiconductor stocks were able to rally higher on the back of a number of tailwinds, but semis got stopped in their tracks as headwinds arose, especially on the geopolitical front. Individual stocks may be able to go against the grain, but Q2 2026 is likely to see more downwards pressure on stocks, unless a solution is found to what is weighing on the sector.

Tailwinds have morphed into headwinds for semis

A previous article at the start of Q1 2026 pointed out how semiconductor stocks look poised for gains because they had a number of powerful tailwinds in their favor, which included accommodation in terms of Fed monetary policy, but, at the same time, these tailwinds had the potential to turn into headwinds. In the end, the article concluded the sector was probably heading higher, but only if tailwinds like Fed policy and AI remained in its corner throughout the whole year.

These comments at the start of 2026 have begun to look rather prescient with one quarter done. For instance, the iShares PHLX Semiconductor ETF (SOXX), an ETF which tracks the semiconductor space, wound up with a gain of 9.13% in Q1 2026, which allowed it to outperform both the SPDR S&P500 ETF (SPY) with a loss of 4.63% and the Invesco QQQ trust (QQQ) with a loss of 6.04%.

However, SOXX was up as much as 22.47% at one point in Q1 2026 and the main reason why it lost most of the quarter’s gains was because geopolitical tensions in the Middle East turned into a full-blown regional war as the U.S. and Iran came to blows at the end of February. As stated in the article at the start of the year, semis look poised to benefit from monetary easing from the Federal Reserve, which included not just lower interest rates, but also the resumption in the buying of Treasury Bills.

However, the war in Iran and the restrictions imposed on the Strait of Hormuz have thrown a spanner into the works by potentially derailing the ability of the Fed to ease since doing so could further inflame inflation, and by extension the cost of living many people are complaining about. It even has the potential of forcing the Fed to do a one-eighty by resorting to monetary tightening to curb rising inflation, which has in past history given growth stocks, which includes semis, all sorts of problems.

Why Q1 2026 could have been worse

It is worth mentioning that while semis as a whole ended up despite losses in March, the final results could have been worse if certain things had gone a little bit differently. For instance, the chart posted earlier shows how SOXX rallied higher since it hit bottom in April 2025, which was the result of a selloff that hit stocks following tariffs and “Liberation Day”.

In fact, not only did SOXX rally higher, but it appears to have followed a trendline higher for the past 12 months or so. Yet there were several instances in which SOXX came close to breaking the trendline by moving below it, including twice in the month of March. For instance, SOXX hit an intraday low of $316.22 on March 9, only to end the day much higher at $336.37.

This is significant because if we retrace what SOXX did beforehand, which saw SOXX rally from the April 2025 low of $148.31 to the February 2026 high of $368.82, as shown in the earlier chart, then the first potential support level or the 23.6% Fibonacci retracement of $148.31 to $368.82 is $316.78, or less than one percent, $0.56 to be exact, removed from $316.22, the March 9 low. In other words, SOXX had started to peak below a support level, which could have led to a break of support, but the timely bounce in the stock allowed it to clear the first challenge by showing that support is standing its ground.

Another challenge occurred later in March, March 30 to be more specific, which saw SOXX hit an intraday low of $307.26, or well below the previously mentioned support at $316.78, although it closed the day somewhat higher at $309.79. This is the lowest the stock has closed at in 2026, which puts it not only below the support provided by the Fibonacci retracement level mentioned earlier, but also below the uptrend, as defined by the trendline in the chart posted, that has been in place since April of last year.

However, another bounce the next day or March 31 allowed SOXX to get back above both the trendline and support provided by a Fibonacci retracement because the stock closed at $328.66 that day, or well above $316.78. More importantly, it is worth mentioning that both of these two dates, March 9 and March 31, which saw SOXX rally 4% and 6%, respectively, on these dates, coincided with major statements from President Trump.

March 31 saw President Trump announce the war with Iran could end in 2-3 weeks as the U.S. would be leaving soon. This is similar to what happened on March 9 when President Trump stated during a press conference that the war with Iran was a short-term excursion that could be finished pretty quickly. The market likes the idea of an imminent end to the war in the Middle East and the market got what it wanted to hear on both dates, which caused stocks to rally, SOXX included.

It is of course possible that it is a coincidence for these statements to have occurred on dates when stocks like SOXX were at a critical junction, close to breaking through support, which likely would have paved the way for more downside. On the other hand, if these events had not occurred, at a specific point on the day they did, and SOXX had not gotten the outside boost when it needed one, SOXX would likely have ended the quarter significantly worse than it did.

The table above shows why Q1 2026 was not a bad quarter for the sector overall as most semis ended the quarter with gains, something that can’t be said of other sectors. Of the 30 stocks in SOXX, 21 ended Q1 2026 up and just 9 finished in the red. On the other hand, most ended the quarter well off their highs as the war in Iran resulted in all but four to lose ground in the month of March.

The four that managed to buck the trend in what was a tough month for semis, and stocks in general, were MRVL, ARM, STM and AMD. MRVL’s performance in particular stood out with a gain of 21.25% in March because if not for the stock gaining in March, which would not have happened without help from NVDA, it would have ended the quarter with losses. In contrast, MU was the worst performer in the list above during March with a loss of 18.07% as some of the air that had build up in memory stocks started to escape.

For the entire quarter, TER ended up as the winner with a gain of 53.16% in Q1 2026, ahead of runner-ups ENTG with a gain of 39.16% and ASX with a gain of 34.66%. At the other end of the spectrum were the losers, which included CRDO with a loss of 34.76%, ALAB with a loss of 34.12% and QCOM with a loss of 24.71% during the quarter.

In the past 12 months, only two, QCOM and SWKS are trading below where they did a year ago as the other 28 were able to trade higher. Both also have the distinction of having steadily fallen in the past year and NXPI came close to it. In contrast, MRVL, STM and ARM were able to steadily add to their gains and NVMI just missed out on achieving this feat.

Still, semis have lost a step. Semis usually outperform the indexes like the S&P500 on the way up, but they underperformed during the selloff that hit most stocks in March. The trend was decidedly positive for semis, but it has become less so more recently. The top gainer on a TTM basis is MU with an impressive gain of 288.81%, for instance, but it has progressively slowed down during the past year, culminating in the 18.07% loss in March.

Memory stocks have done exceptionally well, particularly after memory prices for NAND and DRAM in particular spiked higher in the summer of 2025, but the slowdown could portend an ending of what could turn out to be a bubble in retrospect. This included DRAM prices multiplying by 4-5 or even more times in just a few months.

It does not have to carry over into the AI world as a whole, but this could foreshadow problems for AI as a catalyst because the boom in memory prices was a direct consequence of the boom in AI. It was AI that caused demand for memory to spike, boosting prices, but if AI starts to falter, then that will have negative consequences for the semiconductor sector as AI is core to semiconductor demand, especially if it is combined with headwinds elsewhere.

Why war in the Middle East muddies the outlook for semis

Going into 2026 semis had the backing of two powerful tailwinds, easing in Fed policy and the boom in AI. Both promised to tilt the semiconductor sector higher in the new year, but the outbreak of the war in the Middle East has the potential to upend both these factors to the point that the outlook becomes more of a toss-up at the very least, depending on whether one or both serve as a headwind or tailwind for the sector.

The fighting in the Middle East could affect semis in multiple ways. For instance, while AI has boomed, which had provided all sorts of benefits for many semis, it requires a constant infusion of capital to keep the boom going. The Gulf States like Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates and Qatar were expected to become big contributors of fresh capital flowing into AI, but with restrictions imposed on the Strait of Hormuz, their ability to export energy and generate capital is severely diminished. This could hurt those AI companies that were looking at the Gulf states for funding.

One such project is Stargate UAE, which involves such high-profile names in the AI space like OpenAI and NVDA. The Gulf States were expected to become a destination for more datacenters due to relatively low energy prices and other incentives, but the fighting makes this less likely, which in turn could impact semiconductor demand as the chips needed to be housed in these datacenters may no longer be acquired. Note also that some datacenters already in the region have come under attack.

Furthermore, if the fighting in the Middle East continues, the Gulf states will eventually be confronted with big holes in their budgets, which could force these countries to liquidate some of their assets to raise funds. This could involve the selling of equities, which would work its way down to semis. Instead of a tailwind, semis may be confronted with a lasting headwind due to recent events in the Middle East.

There are other ways for semis to be affected. One involves physical damage to local facilities. INTC, for instance, looks especially vulnerable in this regard as it own several facilities in Israel. INTC could potentially suffer damages well into the billions, which would also come at a bad time for INTC and its efforts to power a turnaround from the setbacks suffered in recent years.

Supply chain disruptions could also become an issue for semis, particularly those directly involved in chipmaking, with the region a major source of inputs like helium. However, of all the potential headwinds, the biggest one with the most potential to affect semis is a change in Fed policy. This is because as a consequence of the outbreak of war in the Middle East, the Fed could shift from the accommodation expected at the start of 2026 to something much less so.

At the start of 2026, the Fed was expected to serve as a tailwind for semis since it was on the path to monetary easing, but the recent events in the Middle East have thrown this outlook into doubt. The Federal Reserve had cut the federal funds rate or FFR by three times in 2025 for a reduction of 75 basis points and more rate cuts were expected in 2026.

However, futures have assigned the highest probability to no rate cuts in 2026, which means the FFR could stay at the current 3.50-3.75%. Futures also project there to be just one rate cut of 25 basis points in 2027. This is actually more pessimistic than the Fed’s own outlook for interest rate policy going by the latest dot plots.

In the worst case, the war in the Middle East continues, which keeps oil prices high and which fuels upward pressure on inflation in the economy. Not only would this make the Fed reluctant to stimulate as the market had come to expect, but it is not impossible for the Fed to be forced to hike rates, or the exact opposite of what semis had counted on. Semis tend to not do well when the Fed starts to tighten monetary policy.

What to expect in Q2 2026

The outlook for semis has become less clear heading into Q2 2026. While individual names may be able to buck the trend, semis as a whole have as much a shot at adding to their gains in Q2 2026 as they have at suffering losses. The determining factor here is that it depends on how the situation in the Middle East evolves.

While some recent comments suggest an end to the fighting in the Middle is not far away, the reality on the ground is that more military forces are being send to the region. This includes ground forces and close air support or CAS aircraft like the A-10, or the type of combat aircraft that works in close conjunction with ground troops. It begs the question why there is a need for more forces if the winding down of the fighting is not far away.

At this time, a continuation of the fighting in the Middle East looks more likely for the simple reason that a halt in the fighting at this point is undesirable for both sides. If, for instance, the war stops at this time, Iran would remain in control of the Hormuz Strait, which would give Iran immense geopolitical power as many nations, especially in the region would have to cater to it, something very likely to be unacceptable to the U.S. and its allies. The U.S. needs to further degrade the ability of Iran to project force and that requires more fighting.

Similarly, Iran would not want what effectively amounts to a ceasefire or a temporary freeze in the fighting just so the U.S. can restart the war when it better suits the Trump Administration, like after the midterm elections. It would better suit Iran to continue the war as long as it can since that would increase the likelihood of undesirable consequences for the Trump Administration.

If we assume continued fighting as the premise, then this suggests semis are probably in for more turbulence in the second quarter, especially in the early part of the second quarter, if not further downside. Volatility is very likely with wild swings possible as the U.S. knows it has to do everything it can to lower the fallout for energy prices and equity markets, just as Iran knows it must bide for time as the effect of higher energy prices starts to impact daily life.

Investor takeaway

It is said that the first casualty in a war is the truth. Both sides in any war have an interest in denying their opponent knowledge of what they intend to do since the odds of success goes up if you have the element of surprise on your side and the enemy is not prepared or not expecting what you plan on doing. Conversely, the odds of failure goes up if the enemy knows what is coming.

This makes trying to predict the road ahead based on news headlines fraught with risk. This applies to both the long and the short side of the trade. Long/short pairs could be a better alternative than directional trades as the latter may get whiplashed by warring parties that have an interest in shaping public perceptions of the war in the Middle East that aligns with their objectives.

It is unwise to make decisions based on public statements from warring parties. This is applicable to whether or not to be a buyer or seller of stocks, especially trades for the short term. It is better to attach more weight to what is happening on the ground, which includes whether more forces are mobilized to be send over or if there is an increase or decrease in attacks from Iran. Both would be a better indicator of where the war is going and by extension what semis can expect to happen, in Q2 2026 and beyond.

If semis are to regain the vigor they displayed in the early part of Q1 2026, a resolution to the conflict in the Middle East will be required. It might be different for individual stocks, but semis as a group are unlikely to do well if there is a lingering uncertainty as to what lies ahead for the semiconductor sector, especially in terms of Fed policy or whether the Fed will ease or even tighten.

The ideal outcome for semis would be for the U.S. to find a way to decisively defeat Iran. Such an outcome would brighten the outlook for semis and likely set them free on the way higher. On the other hand, the longer Iran succeeds in denying the U.S. a clear victory, the more likely it becomes that the semiconductor sector, as tracked by ETFs like SOXX, starts to feel the heat as the consequences from the fighting in the Middle East starts to make its way around.
